Abstract
--------

This study aims to examine digital technology adoption and globalization innovation implications on green sustainable economic growth on Asian Pacific selected countries (Malaysia, Indonesia, Singapore, Philippines, Thailand, [Japan](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/japan "Learn more about Japan from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [India](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/india "Learn more about India from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Korea](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/korea "Learn more about Korea from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), China, New Zealand, Australia) via integrating digital technology adoption (digitalization and digitization), globalization, and [environmental quality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/environmental-valuation "Learn more about environmental quality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This study employed the [Hausman test](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/hausman-test "Learn more about Hausman test from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to determine the appropriate estimation approach between random effect and [fixed effect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/fixed-effects-model "Learn more about fixed effect from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") methods due to the digital adoption index (DAI) data limitation. The Hausman test confirms the random effect method for this analysis and the findings reveal that digital technology adoption promotes economic growth in Asian Pacific countries. Globalization was found an insignificant positive influence on Pacific countries’ economic growth. The average growth rate of green [total factor productivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/total-factor-productivity "Learn more about total factor productivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (GTFP) as a Technology progress of innovation and environmental integration is positive, this might potentially be described by the high contribution of digital technology adoption and from the beneficial effects of labor, capital, CO2, and globalization positives contribution. The significant contribution of this study is filling the gaps via promoting green sustainable economic growth through inclusion of digital technology adoption and globalization in the growth accounting model that ignored. Technology adoption and globalization [internalization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/internalization "Learn more about internalization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") plays a remarkable role to enhance innovations and knowledge and technology transfer, enabling companies to access wide-ranging markets, encouraging [foreign direct investment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/foreign-direct-investment "Learn more about foreign direct investment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), collaboration and smart partnerships along with conveying knowledge within and among the nations in technological creativity, globalization-based technology transfer, and [intellectual property](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/intellectual-property "Learn more about intellectual property from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") development to implement [Sustainable Development Goals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/sustainable-development-goals "Learn more about Sustainable Development Goals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (SDGs).

Introduction
------------

The adoption of digital technology (digitalization and digitization) and globalization have become significant aspects of today's world in facilitating work completion rapidly and reducing costs. Adopting digital technology and globalization are key factors affecting long-term green economic growth. The use of digital technology can improve resource efficiency, reduce emissions, and create new opportunities for green economic growth by boosting communication, improving processes, and encouraging innovation. The adoption of digital technology and globalization have become significant aspects of today’s world in facilitating work completion rapidly and reducing costs. Digital economy-related technological change among others has an impact on [inflation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/inflation "Learn more about inflation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), wages, [labor markets](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/labor-market "Learn more about labor markets from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and production ([Ahmed, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib4)). The process of globalization stimulates innovation and hastens technology transfer, the development of the 1970s within structural transformation was featured by growth in labor productivity as a result of technological advancement ([Skare and Soriano, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib49)). Due to the intensifying economic activity of [digitalization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/digitalization "Learn more about digitalization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the world economy is undergoing a stage of structural and technological change ([Sichoongwe, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib48)). In 2016, 15.5% of the gross domestic product came from the global digital economy ([Cusolito et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib14)). Technological innovations have provided resolves to human necessities and potentially become new drivers of economic growth and social improvement ([Li and Piachaud, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib30)). Globalization has a crucial effect on world competition and productivity-Multi-factor ([Skare and Soriano, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib49)).

Research on technological innovation often concentrates on technology changes in the society's and institute's perspective. Furthermore, approaches to the phenomenon of technological innovation are often used to analyze the benefits ([Park and Choi, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib38)). The transformation in business models from conventional to digital raises several issues and worries about the complications that present-day [industries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/industry "Learn more about industries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") will confront from financial, economic, social, and technological perspectives ([Mura and Donath, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib33)). [Open innovation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/open-innovation "Learn more about Open innovation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can potentially lower obstacles to the [circular economy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/circular-economy "Learn more about circular economy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), however, a minor is understood about the way both areas can work together or how open innovation can help create a more sustainable economy ([Jesus & Jugend 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib27)). Participating in open innovation programs as part of Triple Helix partnerships has the potential to foster economic growth ([Nel and Cook, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib35)). Digital technology has advanced quickly for many years. The global economy has undergone a rapid transformation as a result of the quick uptake of the solutions of mobile and smart digital in [business and society](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/business-and-society "Learn more about business and society from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Virtually everything in our world is powered by digital technology. As a result, productivity is rapidly increasing across all industries, businesses, and societal sectors ([Qiu, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib40)). According to [Tsai et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib52), open innovation could promote reversed knowledge transfer among stakeholders, however not only through the sharing of general knowledge that can be easily "imagined" from any associated organization. The development of new products has drawn the majority of the interest in [SMEs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/medium-sized-firm "Learn more about SMEs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") inward open innovation, but processing or, concurrently, [product innovation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/simultaneous-engineering "Learn more about product innovation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") may also be pursued by SMEs ([Hervas-Oliver et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib23)).

Throughout the course of the last ten years, [Asia's](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/asia "Learn more about Asia's from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") expanding infrastructure and technological capabilities have enabled it to make remarkable progress in human development, literacy, innovation, and economic advancement. Asia has embraced the digital revolution across a range of sectors, including financing, [electronic commerce](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/e-commerce "Learn more about electronic commerce from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), manufacturing, banking, healthcare, and retail ([Roy, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib44)). While Asian companies are demonstrating the way in almost every area of digitalization, several nations are far behind. As a result, there is the widest variation in the adoption of digital technology across Asian economies, as [Hong Kong](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/hong-kong "Learn more about Hong Kong from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") SAR, [Japan](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/japan "Learn more about Japan from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Korea](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/korea "Learn more about Korea from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and Singapore, setting trends worldwide. However, in comparison to their worldwide counterparts, Asian economies are at the cutting edge at any given income level. Furthermore, digitization is speeding up even for the relatively poorer economies of Asia, such as those of Nepal and Cambodia ([Sedik, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib47)). Before the outbreak of the pandemic, Asia accounted for over 50% of all patents worldwide, with 60% of those about digital and computer technologies. Asia has emerged as a major innovator. ([Dabla-Norris et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib15)).

Although on a different scale, scope, and pace, improvements in digital technology have presented economic performers in both developed and developing nations with challenges. Digital technology has challenged established practices and theoretical precepts in businesses and governments, proving that it is genuinely disruptive. It has provided access to information (in actual time), connects individuals to businesses worldwide, opens new markets, supports efforts to hold government accountable, lowers costs, and boosts operational effectiveness and efficiency, providing unprecedented opportunities for economic growth ([Chiemeke and Imafidor, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib13)).

In the globalization context, digital technology has become recognized as one of the most appropriate solutions to assist developing nations in catching up with developed countries. Digital technology advancements help developing economies grow economically by lowering transaction costs and enhancing workers' knowledge and skills ([Bon, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib8)). By conveying foreign know-how and promoting global competition, globalization refines technology adoption ([Skare and Soriano, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib49)). By importing pro-environmental technologies and low energy-consuming production inputs, [international trade](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/international-trade "Learn more about international trade from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") considerably promotes the improvement of [environmental quality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/environmental-valuation "Learn more about environmental quality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Wang et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib54)). According to [Aqib and Zaman (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib6), over the next 10 years, the key [human capital](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/human-capital "Learn more about human capital from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") drivers influencing [carbon emissions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/carbon-dioxide-emission "Learn more about carbon emissions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") will be life expectancy and the net enrolment rate (using an innovation accounting matrix), and the agenda for green development is influenced not only by human capital but also by shifts in the labor market, investments in R&D, and technology that facilitates work.

In this regard, [Ahmed and Elfaki (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib5) estimated a model like this without including digital technology adoption and globalization. Therefore, to fill these gaps, this study internalized digital technology adoption and globalization into the previous structured framework and model based on the notion discussed by [Ahmed (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib4) to capture globalization and technology adoption effects on long-run growth as green technological progress. To the best of our knowledge, this topic has not been covered in any research to this moment. In terms of methodology, a framework and model of productivity of positive and negative external factors have been formulated and display how to quantify variables by the use of secondary data to fill these gaps in the current productivity model through estimating the digital adoption index (DAI) and globalization impacts on growth in the first step. The second phase was proposed to calculate [total factor productivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/total-factor-productivity "Learn more about total factor productivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (TFP) indicators through plugging the estimated regressor parameters into the TFP model to compute productivity factors that have been overlooked in the growth accounting approach. To estimate the digital adoption index (DAI) and globalization impacts on growth, this study followed [Skare and Soriano's (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib49) implication by applying the [Hausman (2015)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib22) test to determine the appropriate estimation approach between random effect and [fixed effect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/fixed-effects-model "Learn more about fixed effect from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") methods due to the digital adoption index (DAI) data limitation. By doing this, the paper contributes considerably via closing the abovementioned gaps in extensive growth theory found the past studies employed [econometric](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/econometrics "Learn more about econometric from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and growth accounting approaches. It’s evident from previous studies that many indicators are used to study the influences of globalization and technology on economic growth. However, there is no consensus on specific indicators to capture globalization and technology effects while considering [total factor productivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/total-factor-productivity "Learn more about total factor productivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (TFP) as technological progress of digital technology adoption and globalization through which globalization is the channel that boosts technology transfer as an exogenous indicator to estimate the contribution of digital technology adoption, globalization, and [pollutants emissions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/pollutant-emission "Learn more about pollutants emissions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") variables quality that termed green TFP as a measurement of integration of innovation, globalization, and climate change.

Literature review
-----------------

The seminal work of [Solow (1956)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib51) introduced the economic growth model that technological progress is exogenous. [Romer (1990)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib43) augmented endogenous technological change through developing a growth model. According to Romer's model, technological change is propelled by the invention of new products as a result of research and development by profit-maximizing business corporations. Technology and globalization concepts and their impact have been measured by different notions and indicators. Technology refers to the application of scientific knowledge to be utilized to attain tangible objectives in a reproducible manner. One strategy that can result in greater success is the outside search for information and its inclusion in the framework of [open innovation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/open-innovation "Learn more about open innovation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Rauter et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib42)). Open innovation can potentially lower obstacles to the [circular economy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/circular-economy "Learn more about circular economy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), however, a minor is understood about the way both areas can work together or how open innovation can help create a more sustainable economy (Jesus & Jugend 2023). According to the open innovation strategy argument, novel concepts could be offered to address the challenge of helping businesses innovate to comply with strict environmental rules while maintaining high productivity and [sustainable development](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about sustainable development from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Hu et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib24)). [Chaston and Scott (2012)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib12) draw a managerial implication suggesting that establishing a robust [organizational learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/organizational-learning "Learn more about organizational learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") culture within the participating [enterprises](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/company "Learn more about enterprises from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") may have a positive impact on the success of implementing an open innovation strategy. The opportunities for open innovation initiatives have expanded meaningfully with the beginning of [digitalization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/digitalization "Learn more about digitalization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The provision and accessibility of enormous amounts of information (big data) and the ability to analyze it in novel systems (artificial intelligence/machine learning) at considerably faster ways (broadband, supercomputers), both stationary and mobile have made new mechanisms for interaction, cooperation, and processing possibility ([Burchardt and Maisch, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib10)). The digital transition is obviously becoming an essential driver of innovation as proven by various companies that are employing digital technologies for handling their processes for innovation ([Urbinati et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib53)). Digitalization is a multifaceted concept, it can be discussed from various angles, including technical, financial, economic, and social issues. Digitalization encompasses, among other things, technologies of platforms and blockchain, artificial intelligence, the [Internet of things](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/internet-of-things "Learn more about Internet of things from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), communication and information connectivity, virtual reality, etc ([Mura and Donath, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib33)). On the other side of this study, the process of globalization stimulates innovation and hasten technology transfer ([Skare and Soriano, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib49)). According to [Rauter et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib42), innovation objectives related to sustainability and economy can be achieved jointly. Diverse entities and organizations that participate in the creation of knowledge at various levels are what open innovation includes ([Yun and Liu, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000155#bib57)). enriched_points.expanded_content: Technological innovations enhance global production, reduce costs, and improve communication, driving economic growth and facilitating the rapid exchange of ideas and resources globally.
Dismantling tariffs and quotas facilitates smoother international trade, enabling countries to access wider markets, thus promoting competition and lowering prices for consumers.
Foreign direct investment fosters economic development by introducing capital, technology, and expertise into local markets, enhancing productivity and creating jobs in host countries.
Cultural exchanges enrich societies by promoting understanding and cooperation among nations, leading to enhanced global collaboration and the sharing of diverse perspectives and ideas.
Robust communication networks enable instant connectivity across borders, facilitating real-time collaboration, information sharing, and the expansion of global business operations.
